behavior_summary: This is a 5-bit sequential Fibonacci LFSR. A 5-bit internal state register updates on each positive edge of clk. If rst is high at the clock edge, the state is synchronously loaded with initial_state. Otherwise, if reinit is high at the clock edge, the state is synchronously loaded with initial_state. Otherwise, if advance is high at the clock edge, the next state is computed from the current state by forming feedback_bit = ^(out_state & taps), then shifting the current state toward the MSB side so that new_state[4:1] = current_state[3:0] and new_state[0] = feedback_bit. Otherwise, the state remains unchanged. The output out_state always reflects the full current 5-bit state, and out always equals out_state[0].
edge_cases: If rst is high on a rising clock edge, state loads initial_state synchronously; there is no asynchronous reset behavior.
If reinit is high on a rising clock edge, state loads initial_state instead of advancing.
If both reinit and advance are high on a rising clock edge, reinit takes priority and the state loads initial_state.

If both rst and reinit are high on a rising clock edge, the spec implies rst has priority because reset behavior is defined separately and is typically highest priority in synchronous logic; both actions load the same value initial_state anyway, so externally the result is identical.
If rst, reinit, and advance are all low on a rising clock edge, state holds its previous value.

The feedback bit is the XOR reduction of the 5-bit vector (current_state & taps); equivalently feedback_bit = (current_state[0]&taps[0]) ^ (current_state[1]&taps[1]) ^ (current_state[2]&taps[2]) ^ (current_state[3]&taps[3]) ^ (current_state[4]&taps[4]).
The shift direction is explicit: current_state[3:0] move to new_state[4:1], current_state[4] is discarded, and feedback_bit is inserted into new_state[0].
No saturation, overflow, or underflow concept applies; state evolution is modulo the 5-bit shift/register update rule.
If taps is 5'b00000, feedback_bit is always 0, so repeated advances eventually shift the register toward 0.
If initial_state is 5'b00000 and the module is reset or reinitialized to zero, subsequent advances keep state at zero for any taps.
Outputs are functions of the registered current state; after a clock edge that changes state, out_state and out reflect the updated state with normal register propagation, not one cycle later.
The spec does not define any special illegal state handling, lockup avoidance, maximal-length guarantee, or seed restrictions.
state_transitions: ANY_STATE -> initial_state when rst is asserted at posedge clk
ANY_STATE -> initial_state when rst is low and reinit is asserted at posedge clk
CURRENT_STATE -> {CURRENT_STATE[3], CURRENT_STATE[2], CURRENT_STATE[1], CURRENT_STATE[0], ^(CURRENT_STATE & taps)} when rst and reinit are low and advance is asserted at posedge clk
CURRENT_STATE -> CURRENT_STATE when rst, reinit, and advance are all low at posedge clk
priorities: When reinit and advance are both asserted, reinit overrides state advancement
out is derived from out_state[0] and should not have independent behavior
mutation_risks: Wrong shift direction, such as shifting toward LSB instead of toward MSB
Inserting feedback bit into MSB instead of LSB
Using current_state[4:1] instead of current_state[3:0] for the shifted portion
Failing to discard current_state[4] during advance
Computing feedback from the wrong source bits or wrong tap alignment
Using OR-reduction, XNOR, or full-vector XOR instead of XOR reduction of (state & taps)
Computing feedback from next state instead of current state
Making advance unconditional so state changes even when advance is low
Implementing rst as asynchronous instead of synchronous
Making out equal to the wrong bit, commonly MSB instead of LSB
Registering out separately and allowing it to lag out_state[0] by one cycle
Loading a constant or zero on reset/reinit instead of initial_state
Using taps during reinit/reset when state should simply load initial_state
Off-by-one-cycle bugs where advance/reinit effects appear one edge early or late
